result:
I keep thinking about the moment a signal becomes a memory.

Not the big, cinematic memories—weddings, birthdays, funerals—but the tiny, almost molecular ones: the way the light looked on the underside of a leaf when you were seven and waiting for your mother to finish talking to a neighbor; the metallic smell of a shopping-cart handle in winter; the hush inside a tent when rain starts to fall and you realize the ground beneath your sleeping bag is no longer entirely solid.  

Each of those is a packet of electricity that somehow survives the nightly demolition derby of sleep. Most evenings the brain’s maintenance crew pries up the floorboards and throws whole stretches of the day into a dumpster. But a few splinters refuse to budge. They get sanded down, re-stained, and nailed back tighter than before. After a decade you can’t tell which part is original grain and which is later varnish. That, I suppose, is what we call identity: a DIY refinishing project that never quite cures.

I like to picture every person as a dimly lit warehouse of such warped planks. We walk up and down the aisles with a flashlight, telling visitors, “Oh, this one came from my first beach trip,” while remaining oblivious to the beam’s narrow cone. Behind us, stacked higher than we can see, are towers of mislabeled boards: the Tuesday we stared too long at a stranger’s wrist; the split-second smell of bus exhaust that primed us to fall in love an hour later. No curator, no card catalogue—just the endless echo of our footsteps and the certainty that somewhere in the dark is the very thing we most need to remember, already cut and planed into something else.

Sometimes the warehouse rebels. A plank slides down, clatters, scares us awake at 3:12 a.m. We sit up, heart racing, unable to name the splinter that did it. The feeling lingers like a word on the tip of the tongue that never arrives. In the morning we blame stress, caffeine, the news. But it was only a memory trying to return to its original shape, popping a nail.

Technology keeps promising to back the whole place up: cloud servers, neural implants, perfect digital twins. I’m skeptical. A memory without forgetting is just surveillance. We need the rot, the warp, the accidental polish of thumbs. The child who remembers every shoelace she ever tied would never finish crossing the street.

Besides, the most important part of recollection may be the gap itself—the moment when the plank you’re standing on creaks and you instinctively shift weight. That micro-hesitation is the sound of the future being carved. A perfect archive would freeze the board, eliminate torque, and thereby erase the next step.

So I nurse my imperfect floors. I let the rain smell and the leaf light glimmer and the shopping-cart handle chill revisit me in no particular order. They arrive uninvited, announce no lesson, leave without saying goodbye. And somewhere in that quiet traffic I keep being rebuilt, nail by nail, splinter by splinter, a renovation that will never pass inspection—and therefore stays alive.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
